mechanism: Goldenseal contains isoquinoline alkaloids (including berberine), but oral goldenseal may yield low berberine exposure relative to isolated-compound studies. Mechanistic findings should not be assumed to translate directly to clinical outcomes.
safetyNotes: Short-term oral use around 3 g/day has been used in small studies without serious harms, but longer-term safety is uncertain. Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding and in infants.
description: Hydrastis canadensis is a North American plant used in supplements, historically for respiratory and GI complaints, but robust human efficacy trials are lacking.
dosage: Small studies have used about 3 g/day short term; there is no well-established long-term dosing standard.
interactions: May alter drug disposition; NCCIH describes reduced metformin exposure when combined with goldenseal extract.
Potential interaction risk with medicines processed by CYP pathways; medication review is advised before use.
preparation: Typically sold as rhizome/root powders, capsules, and tincture/extract products.
region: Native to northeastern United States and southeastern Canada.
sideEffects: potential liver toxicity with long use
summary: Goldenseal has limited clinical evidence; NCCIH reports insufficient evidence to determine usefulness for any health condition.
